metadata.summary: Run a structured RAG evaluation pipeline: single-question eval, batch eval, metric aggregation (faithfulness, answer relevance, context relevance, precision@5, MRR), and JSON export.

content.title: RAG Evaluation Pipeline
content.summary: A complete evaluation pipeline runs each test-set question through the production RAG pipeline, computes retrieval metrics (precision@5, recall@5, MRR, hit rate) and generation metrics (faithfulness, answer relevance, context relevance) via LLM scoring or RAGAS, aggregates results, and exports to JSON. Human review of the aggregated report is required before any pipeline change is merged to production.

why.p: Ad-hoc quality checks catch only regressions that are obviously visible in a single response. A systematic pipeline runs every test question, separates retrieval failures from generation failures via distinct metric families, and produces a versioned JSON artifact that can be diffed across pipeline changes. Without this separation it is impossible to know whether a drop in faithfulness is caused by the retriever returning wrong chunks or by the LLM ignoring correct ones.

list.item: Before deploying a RAG system to production — establishing baseline quality scores.
After any pipeline change (chunking strategy, top-K, model swap) to detect regressions.
When the test set has at least 20 questions — smaller sets are dominated by statistical noise.
Weekly batch evaluation on a sampled subset in production to catch quality drift.
High-frequency real-time evaluation of every query in production — use lightweight metrics (latency, hit rate, user signals) instead; reserve LLM eval for sampled batches.
When ground truth answers are unavailable — only faithfulness and answer_relevancy are computable without ground truth; RAGAS context_precision and context_recall require ground truth.

rule.statement: Run evaluation on every config change, not just weekly. Even chunking parameter tweaks can degrade precision@5 significantly.
rule.rationale: Small parameter changes (chunk_size from 500 to 1000, top_k from 5 to 3) compound: each one looks minor in isolation but a sequence of unvalidated changes can silently degrade quality by 20% over a sprint.

rule.statement: Establish numeric quality gates before deployment: e.g., faithfulness > 0.8, precision@5 > 0.7. Without gates, evaluation is informational only and rarely acts as a hard blocker.
rule.rationale: Quality gates transform evaluation from a passive report into an active CI gate. The gate values must be set from empirical baseline runs on the test set before the first deployment.

rule.statement: RAGAS contexts field expects list[list[str]] (list of lists per question), not list[str]. Passing a flat list causes silent incorrect metric computation.
rule.rationale: The RAGAS library does not raise a type error on a flat list; it silently computes wrong context_precision/recall values, producing artificially high scores that mask retrieval failures.

rule.statement: RAGAS evaluate() calls OpenAI internally by default. Ensure OPENAI_API_KEY is set in the environment before invoking; a missing key causes a cryptic error, not a clear auth error.
rule.rationale: The environment check should be the first step in any eval runner script to avoid wasted runs that fail after consuming tokens.

section.name: evaluator-class
section.p: The RAGEvaluator class wraps a production RAG pipeline, runs single-question or batch evaluation, computes both retrieval and generation metrics, and exports results to JSON. The evaluate_single method is the core unit; evaluate_batch iterates the test set and calls aggregate_metrics.

example.title: Complete RAGEvaluator pipeline

section.name: ragas-integration
section.p: RAGAS is the gold-standard library for RAG evaluation. It requires a HuggingFace Dataset format with columns: question, answer, contexts (list of lists), and ground_truth. The evaluate() call returns a dict of metric scores.

example.title: Minimal RAGAS integration
example.code:
from ragas import evaluate from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall from datasets import Dataset
def run_ragas_eval(questions, answers, contexts, ground_truths): """ questions: list[str] answers: list[str] contexts: list[list[str]] — list of retrieved chunks per question ground_truths: list[str] """ dataset = Dataset.from_dict({ "question": questions, "answer": answers, "contexts": contexts, "ground_truth": ground_truths }) result = evaluate( dataset, metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall] ) return dict(result) # {"faithfulness": 0.87, "answer_relevancy": 0.91, ...}

example.title: Flat contexts list — silent wrong metrics
example.code: # WRONG: flat list of strings, not list of lists contexts = ["chunk1 text", "chunk2 text", "chunk3 text"] # RAGAS silently computes wrong context_precision/recall values dataset = Dataset.from_dict({ "question": questions, "answer": answers, "contexts": contexts, # must be list[list[str]] "ground_truth": ground_truths })
example.note: No exception is raised. Scores are wrong. Always use list[list[str]].

section.name: human-evaluation
section.p: Automated LLM evaluators have biases and disagree with human judgment on 20-30% of cases. A human evaluation interface collects factual correctness, relevance, context support, and overall quality scores to calibrate automated metrics and catch systematic LLM evaluator failures.

example.title: Human evaluation interface
example.code:
def human_evaluation_interface( questions: List[str], answers: List[str], contexts: List[List[str]] ): """ Interface for human evaluation.
Guidelines: 1. Is the answer factually correct? (Yes/No) 2. Is the answer relevant to the question? (1-5 scale) 3. Is the answer supported by context? (Yes/No/Partial) 4. Overall quality (1-5 scale) """ results = [] for q, a, c in zip(questions, answers, contexts): print(f"\nQuestion: {q}") print(f"Answer: {a}") print(f"Context: {c[:200]}...")
rating = { "factually_correct": input("Factually correct? (y/n): "), "relevance": int(input("Relevance (1-5): ")), "supported": input("Supported by context? (y/n/p): "), "overall": int(input("Overall quality (1-5): ")) } results.append(rating)
return results

section.name: cost-management
section.p: LLM-based evaluation at scale is expensive. At $0.01 per eval, 10K questions = $100. Budget-based sampling evaluates all high-priority cases first, then random-samples the remainder to fit within a dollar budget. Use this for routine CI checks; run the full test suite only for release decisions.

example.title: Budget-based evaluation sampling
example.code:
def cost_efficient_evaluation( test_set: List[dict], budget_dollars: float, cost_per_eval: float = 0.01 ): """ Evaluate within budget using smart sampling. """ max_evals = int(budget_dollars / cost_per_eval)
if len(test_set) <= max_evals: # Evaluate all return test_set
# Sample strategically # 1. All high-priority cases high_priority = [t for t in test_set if t.get("priority") == "high"]
# 2. Representative sample of others remaining = max_evals - len(high_priority) others = [t for t in test_set if t.get("priority") != "high"] import random sampled = random.sample(others, min(remaining, len(others)))
return high_priority + sampled

section.name: evaluation-frequency
section.p: Run evaluation at the right frequency per stage to balance quality assurance against cost.
section.note: Development: every change, Precision@5 + Faithfulness. Pre-deployment: full test suite, all RAGAS metrics. Production: real-time latency + hit rate + user signals. Weekly: batch eval on sample.

content.prompt: You are a RAG evaluator. Given: question, answer, context (retrieved chunks), ground_truth. Compute: - faithfulness (0-1): fraction of answer claims supported by context - answer_relevance (0-1): how completely the answer addresses the question - context_relevance (0-1): fraction of context that is relevant to the question Return: {"faithfulness": X, "answer_relevance": Y, "context_relevance": Z, "explanation": "..."}

antipattern.description: Relying only on automated LLM metrics without any human validation pass.
antipattern.reason: LLM evaluators disagree with human judgment on 20-30% of cases. Automated scores can be gamed by verbosity (high answer_relevance for long non-committal answers) and by context-parroting (high faithfulness for answers that copy context without reasoning).

antipattern.description: Treating evaluation as a one-time pre-deployment gate rather than a continuous CI fixture.
antipattern.reason: RAG quality degrades gradually as the corpus updates, the retrieval index drifts, or the LLM provider updates model weights. Without continuous evaluation, regressions are only discovered when users complain.
